research_goal: Understand why internal teams aren't adopting Pulse, our internal analytics platform.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.text: Thanks for making time, Raj. You're one of the heaviest Pulse users I could find, so I want to hear the unfiltered version. How would you describe your experience with it?

turns.speaker: interviewee
turns.text: Honestly? Love-hate. I'm in Pulse every single day, I push it harder than anyone on my team. But the ceiling is low. I run into the edges constantly, and the edges are where my actual work lives.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.text: Tell me about one of those edges from this week.

turns.speaker: interviewee
turns.text: Tuesday. I was doing a cross-team analysis comparing our funnel against the onboarding team's. And I keep hitting permission walls, I can't see other teams' data even when I need it for a cross-team analysis. So I filed an access request, waited two days, got half of it approved. The analysis was already stale by then.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.text: How often does that access friction come up for you?

turns.speaker: interviewee
turns.text: Weekly, easily. Almost every interesting question I have is cross-team, and that's exactly the data I'm walled off from. The permissions are scoped per team, so the moment I reach across a boundary I'm stuck filing tickets and pinging admins. It kills the momentum every time.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.text: Set permissions aside for a second. When you do have the data, how does Pulse hold up?

turns.speaker: interviewee
turns.text: That's the other half of it. Any complex query I write just times out. The second I join more than a couple of tables or look at a wide date range, it spins and then dies. So I end up dumping raw data into my own notebook and doing the real work in pandas, which kind of defeats the whole point of having Pulse.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.text: If you're already escaping to a notebook, what keeps you coming back to Pulse at all?

turns.speaker: interviewee
turns.text: The shared dashboards and the fact that it's the source of truth, when it works. And here's the thing people get wrong about adoption: I'd actually use it if it gave me more query power and bigger limits, not fewer. Everyone assumes power users churn because it's too complicated. For me it's the opposite, I churn because it's too constrained. Oh, and a small gripe, I wish dashboards had version history so I could see what changed when a number suddenly moves.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.text: If you could change exactly one thing tomorrow, what would it be?

turns.speaker: interviewee
turns.text: Raise the query timeout ceiling and let me opt into bigger limits, and fix the cross-team access so I'm not walled off from the data I need. Give me more room, not guardrails. Do that and I'd move my whole workflow into Pulse tomorrow instead of treating it as a glorified export button.

summary: Raj, a power user, is frustrated that Pulse walls him off from cross-team data and times out on complex queries, and argues he'd adopt it fully if it offered more query power and bigger limits rather than tighter constraints.
